fact: August 28 sits exactly 240 days into the year - that's like counting to 240 Mississippi's without stopping!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is when many spiders start getting HUGE and building massive webs because they're preparing for autumn mating season.
The sun rises about 6 minutes later than it did just one week ago - summer is sneaking away while you sleep!
Monarch butterflies in North America are going absolutely bonkers right now, starting their epic 3,000-mile journey to Mexico.
This date falls smack in the middle of Virgo season, when the constellation Virgo dominates the night sky like a cosmic perfectionist.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, August 28 is when wattle trees explode with bright yellow flowers, making the landscape look like it's covered in sunshine pom-poms.
The Atlantic Ocean is at its absolute warmest temperature of the entire year around this time - perfect for hurricanes to power up like nature's blenders!
Crickets are chirping their loudest on August nights, and you can actually tell the temperature by counting their chirps and doing simple math.
This is peak time for the Perseid meteor shower's little cousin - random shooting stars still zip across the sky from leftover space debris.
In northern forests, squirrels enter full panic mode around August 28, frantically burying nuts like tiny, furry doomsday preppers.
The Earth is tilting away from the sun so fast that daylight shrinks by about 2-3 minutes every single day in most places.
Late August is when many trees start pumping sugar down to their roots instead of their leaves, which is why autumn colors will start showing up soon.
